original_hypothesis.title: Bribe.earned Unbounded Loop DoS Permanently Locks User Rewards
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Bribe.earned() iterates over all unclaimed epochs in a for loop: `for (uint256 i = 0; i < numEpochs; i++)`. With WEEK=1800 seconds (30 minutes per epoch), 48 epochs accumulate per day. If a user doesn't claim bribes for even a moderate period, the loop's SLOAD-heavy iterations (checkpoint binary search per epoch) exceed block gas limits. The user's rewards become permanently unclaimable with no partial claim mechanism.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Each iteration performs getPriorBalanceIndex (binary search through checkpoints - multiple SLOADs) and getPriorSupplyIndex (another binary search). At ~48 epochs/day, 30 days creates 1440 iterations. Even with Avalanche's higher gas limits, this will exceed limits due to the O(n*log(m)) complexity where n=epochs and m=checkpoints.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unbounded for loop: numEpochs = (epochStart(block.timestamp) - _currTs) / WEEK; with WEEK=1800 this grows 48x per day

preconditions: User does not claim bribe rewards for extended period (days to weeks)
User has active voting position earning bribes

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: numEpochs = (epochStart(now) - lastEarnEpoch) / 1800; each iteration does 2 binary searches through checkpoints
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Natural occurrence - any user who doesn't claim frequently loses access to rewards
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Permanently locked rewards effectively redistribute to other claimants or remain in contract

economic_questions: What is the exact gas cost per earned() iteration on Avalanche?
At what numEpochs does the function exceed Avalanche block gas limit?
How many users are likely to be affected based on typical claim frequency?
validation_requirements: Gas benchmark earned() with increasing numEpochs
Identify Avalanche block gas limit and calculate maximum claimable epochs

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All unclaimed bribe rewards for affected users
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: N/A - DoS, not direct profit extraction

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Continuous - affects any user not claiming frequently
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Progressive loss of user trust as rewards become unclaimable

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Any voter who delays bribe claims beyond gas limit threshold
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% of unclaimed bribe rewards permanently locked
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Material - depends on bribe values and claim frequency

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: WEEK=1800, Avalanche gas limit ~8M-15M, SLOAD cost per iteration
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Gas profiling of earned() at various numEpochs values
investigation_priorities.detection_difficulty: low - users will notice failed claims

validation_result.impact_summary: Users who do not claim their bribe rewards for approximately 25-30 days will have their rewards permanently locked due to block gas limit exhaustion.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The vulnerability is caused by the combination of a very short epoch duration and an unbounded loop used to calculate accrued rewards. In `BlackTimeLibrary.sol`, `WEEK` is hardcoded to `1800` seconds (30 minutes), resulting in 48 epochs per day. 

When a user claims rewards via `Bribe.getReward()`, it calls `Bribe.earned()`. The `earned()` function calculates `numEpochs` as the difference between the current epoch and the epoch of the user's last claim, divided by `WEEK`. It then iterates `numEpochs` times in a `for` loop. 

During each iteration, the loop calls `getPriorBalanceIndex()` and `getPriorSupplyIndex()`. Both of these functions perform binary searches over storage arrays (`checkpoints` and `supplyCheckpoints`), requiring multiple `SLOAD` operations per iteration. At approximately 6 cold `SLOAD`s per iteration (~12,600 gas), a user who waits 30 days to claim will trigger 1,440 iterations, costing over 18 million gas. This exceeds the Avalanche C-Chain block gas limit (typically 15M). 

Because `getReward()` always attempts to calculate and claim rewards up to `block.timestamp` and offers no pagination or partial claim mechanism, once the required gas exceeds the block limit, the transaction will permanently revert with an Out-of-Gas error, locking the user's rewards forever.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The evidence directly supports the hypothesis. `BlackTimeLibrary.sol` hardcodes `WEEK = 1800`. `Bribes.sol` contains the unbounded `for` loop in `earned()` that iterates based on `WEEK`. `getReward()` lacks any mechanism to limit the number of epochs processed in a single transaction.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The `for` loop iterates `numEpochs` times. Because `WEEK` is only 1800 seconds, `numEpochs` grows by 48 every day. Each iteration performs multiple state reads via `getPriorBalanceIndex` and `getPriorSupplyIndex`, quickly leading to block gas limit exhaustion.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet:
library BlackTimeLibrary {
    uint256 internal constant WEEK = 1800;
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The epoch duration is hardcoded to 30 minutes, which causes the unbounded loop in `earned()` to grow at an unsustainable rate of 48 iterations per day.

The `getReward` function calls `earned()` and updates `lastEarn` to `block.timestamp`. There is no parameter to specify a maximum number of epochs to process, meaning users cannot perform partial claims to bypass the gas limit.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When a user attempts to claim bribes, `GaugeManager` calls `Bribe.getReward()`, which in turn calls `Bribe.earned()`. Inside `earned()`, the code calculates `numEpochs` based on the time elapsed since the last claim divided by `WEEK` (1800 seconds). It then enters a `for` loop that runs `numEpochs` times. Inside the loop, it calls `getPriorBalanceIndex` and `getPriorSupplyIndex`, both of which perform binary searches over storage arrays. If the user hasn't claimed in ~25 days, `numEpochs` exceeds 1200, and the cumulative gas cost of the storage reads exceeds the block gas limit, causing the transaction to revert.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: The vulnerability relies on the hardcoded constant `WEEK = 1800` in `BlackTimeLibrary.sol`, which is compiled directly into the bytecode and is not a deployment configuration parameter.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is a natural occurrence rather than an active exploit. A user stakes their veNFT and earns bribes but forgets or chooses not to claim them for a month. When they finally attempt to claim via `GaugeManager.claimBribes()`, the transaction runs out of gas. Because there is no way to claim a subset of the pending epochs, the user's rewards are permanently locked in the Bribe contract.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH. Users will permanently lose access to their earned bribe rewards if they do not claim them frequently. This breaks core protocol functionality and results in a permanent loss of yield for affected users.
recommended_mitigations: Implement a pagination mechanism in `getReward` and `earned` that allows users to specify a maximum number of epochs to process in a single transaction, updating `lastEarn` only up to the processed epoch.

If `WEEK = 1800` is strictly for testing, ensure it is changed to `604800` (7 days) for production deployment. However, even with 7-day epochs, pagination is recommended to prevent DoS for users who do not claim for several years.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy the protocol with `WEEK = 1800`. 2. Create a gauge and a bribe contract. 3. Have a user vote on the gauge to deposit into the bribe contract. 4. Fast forward the blockchain time by 30 days (1440 epochs). 5. Attempt to call `claimBribes` for the user. 6. Observe the transaction reverting with an Out-of-Gas error.
uncertainties: It is highly likely that `WEEK = 1800` is a leftover testing artifact, as 30-minute epochs are extremely unusual for ve(3,3) protocols. If changed to 7 days before deployment, the severity drops significantly, though the architectural flaw (unbounded loop) remains.
additional_testing_needed: Profile the exact gas cost of `earned()` per iteration on the target network (Avalanche) to determine the precise threshold of days before a claim becomes impossible.
